title: Elle Programming Language
description: A Lisp with deep static analysis: binding resolution, capture analysis, and signal inference at compile time

content.text: Elle is a Lisp. What separates it from other Lisps is the depth of its static analysis: full binding resolution, capture analysis, and signal inference happen at compile time, before any code runs. This gives Elle a sound signal system, fully hygienic macros, colorless concurrency via fibers, and deterministic memory management — all derived from the same analysis pass.

items: **Fibers** — independent execution contexts with their own stack, call frames, signal mask, and heap. Cooperative and explicitly resumed. When a fiber finishes, its heap is freed in O(1) — no GC pause, no reference counting.

**Signals** — typed, cooperative flow-control interrupts. A signal is a keyword (`:error`, `:log`, `:abort`, or user-defined) that a fiber emits to its parent. The compiler infers which functions can emit signals.
**Hygienic Macros** — full metaprogramming with `defmacro`, quasiquote/unquote, `gensym`, and scope sets
**Colorless Concurrency** — fibers, coroutines, and OS threads from the same primitives
**Pattern Matching** — `match` with destructuring, struct tags, wildcards, and `& rest`
**FFI** — call C functions directly with `ffi/native` and `ffi/defbind`
**Bytecode Compilation** — programs are compiled to bytecode with optional JIT

content.text: A fiber yields progress updates; the parent drives it to completion:

(defn process-items [items]
  (each item items
    (emit :progress {:item item :result (* item item)})))

(def f (fiber/new (fn [] (process-items [1 2 3])) |:progress|))

(forever
  (fiber/resume f)
  (if (= (fiber/status f) :paused)
    (print "progress:" (fiber/value f))
    (break)))
When the fiber finishes, its entire heap is freed in O(1).
Elle ships with comprehensive design documents and runnable examples:

items: [Language Guide](language-guide.html) — data types, functions, and control flow
[Documentation](documentation.html) — language reference, design documents, and contributor guides
[Examples](examples.html) — 20+ runnable programs covering every major feature
[Standard Library](stdlib-reference.html) — all built-in functions, auto-generated from runtime metadata
